statement: A soccer ball is kicked from flat ground at 20 m/s and 30° above horizontal. Find the horizontal and vertical components of the launch velocity, the total time the ball spends in the air, and the horizontal range — the distance from kick to landing.

vx.prompt: What is the horizontal component of the launch velocity?
vx.hint: The horizontal component is the projection of the velocity vector onto the x-axis: v₀ times the cosine of the angle.
commonMistakes: Using sin instead of cos for the horizontal component
Using the angle in degrees instead of radians in the trig function
Forgetting to multiply by v₀ and writing just cos(θ)

vy.prompt: What is the vertical component of the launch velocity?
vy.hint: The vertical component is the projection onto the y-axis: v₀ times the sine of the angle.
commonMistakes: Using cos instead of sin for the vertical component
Confusing which component stays constant during flight (it is the horizontal one)

t_flight.prompt: How long is the ball in the air?
t_flight.hint: Set y(t) = 0 and solve. The non-zero solution is T = 2 · v₀ · sin(θ) / g.
commonMistakes: Using T = v₀ / g instead of T = 2·v₀·sin(θ) / g
Forgetting the factor of 2 that comes from the ball rising and then falling
Dividing by g² instead of g

R.prompt: What is the horizontal range — distance from launch to landing?
R.hint: Range = horizontal velocity × time of flight = v₀²·sin(2θ) / g. Remember sin(2θ) = 2·sin(θ)·cos(θ).
commonMistakes: Writing v₀·sin(2θ)/g instead of v₀²·sin(2θ)/g (forgetting to square v₀)
Using sin(θ) instead of sin(2θ) in the range formula

Multiplying by the full time T when only half of it applies to the horizontal trip — this error disappears once you use the compact formula, but surfaces if the student tries R = vₓ · T manually and makes an arithmetic slip

finalAnswerProse: The ball lands 35.3 m from the kick point after spending 2.04 s in the air.
walkthrough:
Start by splitting the initial velocity into components. The horizontal component vₓ = 20·cos(30°) ≈ 17.32 m/s stays constant for the entire flight because no horizontal force acts (air is off). The vertical component vy = 20·sin(30°) = 10 m/s decreases under gravity at a rate of g ≈ 9.807 m/s² per second.

The ball is in the air until it returns to the same height it left. Setting y(t) = 0 gives two solutions: t = 0 (launch) and t = T = 2·vy/g = 2·10/9.807 ≈ 2.04 s. That is the time of flight.

Range is horizontal velocity times time of flight: R = vₓ · T = 17.32 · 2.04 ≈ 35.3 m. Equivalently, the compact form R = v₀²·sin(2θ)/g = 400·sin(60°)/9.807 ≈ 35.3 m. Both routes give the same answer — use whichever you find more memorable.

Note: 30° is not the optimum. Maximum range occurs at 45°, where sin(2θ) = sin(90°) = 1. Any angle between 0° and 90° gives a shorter range. Complementary angles — 30° and 60° — give exactly the same range, just via different trajectories.
